title: RA.1022 KAVARI

translation.content: Don't expect KAVARI to take anything too seriously. The Liverpool-born, Glasgow-based artist thrives on contradiction: a pop-adjacent instinct colliding with a love of discomfort, abrasion and noise. Her work twists familiar forms until they snarl—music that gestures toward the mainstream while refusing its rules entirely. After years of releasing independently, 2026 marks a new chapter with PLAGUE MUSIC, her debut on XL, out in February. But if that suggests any kind of conforming, think again. KAVARI's instincts remain the same: push harder, strip things back, make it stranger. It's no surprise, then, that she's earned the support of fellow mould-breakers like Aphex Twin, Ethel Cain and Hudson Mohawke. Of her RA Mix, she shrugs: "I honestly don't remember making it." That irreverence is audible. Disembodied voices mutter club-floor mantras—"spit in the DJ's drink," "that floor pill will give you immortality"—as she drags grime, drum & bass and dubstep through distortion, friction and collapse. Basslines buckle, vocals burn away, tracks frequently flare into noise. There are curveballs too (bet you didn't expect to hear Fred again..) before the rug is pulled again. Characterised by sudden drops and a prankish sense of misdirection, RA.1022 keeps you slightly off-balance throughout. If that all sounds chaotic, well, that's kind of the point. The aim is to unsettle, not just through brute force, but through the unexpected. Once you find your footing, the mix reveals itself as genuinely thrilling dance music, far removed from convention. Because nobody gets anywhere interesting without ruffling a few feathers. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Not sleeping. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? It was recorded in my friend's London bedroom at 4 AM while I was on a ton of Ambien. I honestly don't remember making any of it. What are you looking forward to in the near future? My cat's birthday. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Politics is theatre. Focus on the systems at play and their motives, read the history books and pay attention to action. Don't get caught up in the circus. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Bloodsport in Glasgow. It was the first club night I played where I felt I found my sound as a DJ. SYPHEN in London too, one of the best club nights I've played and also attended—best lineups, best organisation and a highlight of underground club culture in the UK. This week marks your debut release on XL with PLAGUE MUSIC. For someone who's been very honest about the realities of working independently, what has the shift been like? It's been an interesting learning experience. Having been independent my entire career, having XL reach out and want to support my work felt surreal. I was apprehensive at first because I really dislike anyone interfering with what I do. Having to work under other's timings hurts my head a little, but it's been very satisfying having the backing to execute my vision to a higher extent. The XL team is brilliant. This is only the beginning. The rabbit hole only goes deeper into wonderland. A lot of your work lives in darker emotional spaces, but your online presence is often very dry and funny. How do those two sides of you relate, if at all? If you don't laugh, you'll cry. The jester must entertain the court even if he knows the castle is crumbling. I have quite a pessimistic worldview and also no power to change it. So I'm just laughing my way through it and channeling my frustration about it in my music. What else is there to do? After a year of doing radio on NTS, has the way you think about pacing or storytelling changed when you sit down to make a mix like this? My NTS show is me showing my music taste super objectively. When I make mixes I just care about it sounding good and having a good flow of energy throughout, regardless of who made the song, what the song is, or if it's new or old. I used to try to make mixes "stylistic," which is more about all the songs having a similar vibe / genre—now I just play what I want. I feel it tells a better story about me, my inner world and one I want to live in.
translation.blurb: Ahead of her XL debut, the Liverpudlian provocateur delivers 60 minutes of blistering explorations across the hardcore continuum.
